what do you mean by this: Geneva compounds this with a unique additional layer: over 40% of the population are expats. Navigating Swiss healthcare as a foreigner, understanding what kind of doctor you need, what is covered by insurance, who speaks English — is completely opaque. Nobody has built anything to help them. and what do you mean by has no expat-specific onboarding or English-first experience

Mar 25
2 / 2
Deciphered two interconnected business plan concepts regarding market gaps

What "40% expats, nobody built for them" means

Geneva is genuinely unusual. It hosts the UN, WHO, Red Cross, CERN, and hundreds of multinational companies. That pulls in a massive international population — people from France, the UK, the US, India, Brazil, everywhere. These people arrive in Geneva and immediately hit a wall with healthcare.

Here's what it actually looks like in practice:

When a British person moves to Geneva they don't know that you need a "médecin de famille" as your primary doctor before you can see a specialist. They don't know what Lamal or Assura or Swica is or what their insurance actually covers. They Google "doctor Geneva" and get a list of clinics entirely in French. They try to book, the website is in French, they call, someone answers in French, they stumble through it or give up. They don't know which doctors speak English. They don't know that physio requires a referral. They don't know the difference between an urgence and a cabinet médical.

This is a real, daily frustration for hundreds of thousands of people in Geneva. And right now there is genuinely no app or product that walks a newcomer through this. OneDoc exists but it is entirely designed for someone who already understands the Swiss system.

What "no expat onboarding or English-first experience" means for OneDoc

When you open OneDoc it assumes you know what you're doing. It asks you to search by specialty — but if you just arrived from London and your knee hurts you don't know if you need an orthopedist, a physiotherapist, or a general practitioner first. It doesn't tell you. It doesn't filter by language spoken. It doesn't explain insurance. The interface defaults to French. There is no "new to Switzerland?" moment anywhere in the product.

What LOKKT could do differently

Your app could have a simple one-time onboarding screen for new users:

"Just arrived in Switzerland?" — yes or no.

If yes: explain in plain English how the system works in three sentences, ask what language they prefer, ask what insurance they have, and then show them only clinics that match. Flag English-speaking doctors with a small icon. Show which clinics accept their insurance type.

That single flow — which would take maybe two weeks to build — immediately makes LOKKT the only product in Switzerland that actually serves 40% of Geneva's population properly. OneDoc has 13,000 healthcare professionals and still hasn't done this. That's your opening.

what is a GP

Mar 25

GP stands for General Practitioner — it's just a regular family doctor. The first person you see for almost anything medical.

In Practice

If your knee hurts, you're feeling sick, you need a prescription, you want a checkup, you're not sure what's wrong — you go to a GP first. They're the generalist who handles everyday health problems and then refers you to a specialist if needed.

In Switzerland they're sometimes called a médecin généraliste in French or Hausarzt in German. Same thing, different language.

The Chain In Switzerland

GP → diagnoses the problem, treats what they can, writes referrals Specialist → dermatologist, cardiologist, orthopaedist etc — you usually need a GP referral first Hospital → for emergencies or complex procedures

Why It Matters For LOKKT

GPs are your highest volume clinic type. People see their GP multiple times a year — way more frequently than a dentist or a specialist. Getting 10 GP practices in Geneva on LOKKT is worth more than 30 niche specialists in terms of booking volume and patient stickiness.

They're also the hardest appointment to get in Switzerland. Waiting lists are long, many GPs aren't taking new patients, and finding one who speaks English is a real challenge — especially for expats. Solving that problem is genuinely valuable and is exactly where LOKKT should lead its patient-facing marketing.

is it possible for  me to bring all the information from this claude account to another

Mar 26
Identified question about transferring account data between platforms

Unfortunately not in a seamless way — here's the honest breakdown.

What Claude Does and Doesn't Save

Claude doesn't automatically remember conversations between sessions. Each new conversation starts fresh. There's no built-in way to transfer a full conversation history from one account to another automatically.

What You Can Do

Option 1 — Download this conversation On Claude.ai you can export your conversation. Look for the download or export option in the conversation menu. It saves as a text or JSON file. Not the prettiest format but everything is there.

Option 2 — Save the business plan document You already have the full LOKKT business plan as a Word document downloaded. That contains everything strategic from this conversation in a clean format. That's your most important asset from this session.

Option 3 — Copy and paste key sections Select the parts of this conversation you most want to keep — the competitor research, the OneDoc breakdown, the Doctolib story — and paste them into a Google Doc or Notion page for reference.

Option 4 — Use Claude's Memory feature If you enable Memory in Claude settings, Claude can remember key facts about you and your projects across conversations on the same account. It won't transfer to a new account but it means you won't have to re-explain LOKKT every time on this account.

My Honest Recommendation

Keep this account. Everything you've built today — the research, the plan, the logo process — lives here. Starting a new account means starting from scratch conversationally.

If you're worried about losing this, download the business plan document now and save this conversation via the export option. Those two things together capture everything.

yes its just because i put the wrong age on my phone for claude and now i cant use it

Mar 26

Ah that makes sense — annoying but fixable.

The easiest solutions:

Option 1 — Use Claude on desktop/browser instead Go to claude.ai on a laptop or computer and log in with the same account. The age restriction is likely only triggering on the mobile app. The browser version should work fine and you have access to everything including this conversation.

Option 2 — Fix the age on your phone The age restriction usually comes from your App Store account age, not Claude itself. To fix it:

  • On iPhone: go to Settings → Screen Time → Content & Privacy Restrictions → check if age restrictions are set
  • Or go to your Apple ID settings and check your date of birth — if it's set wrong it affects all apps
  • On Android: Google Play has a similar parental controls section

Option 3 — Contact Claude support Go to support.claude.ai and explain the situation. They can help sort account-level issues. It's a straightforward fix for their team.

Before You Switch Accounts

Make sure you have:

  • The LOKKT business plan Word document downloaded ✅
  • This conversation bookmarked or exported
  • The logo saved from Canva

That way even if you end up on a different account you haven't lost anything. The document has everything — the research, the plan, the pricing, the competitor analysis, all of it. You're not starting from zero.

new day new ideas. Good morning Claude, lets work on Lokkt, what are we thinking of doing today?

5:04 PM
Welcomed returning collaborator and charted LOKKT project trajectory

Good morning! Great to be back on LOKKT.

You're in a solid position — brand done, plan done, logo done. The natural next moves from here are:

The most important thing right now is the waitlist. Before the app exists, before anything else, you need a page where clinics can sign up. One simple website — lokkt.ch — that captures interest and starts building your founding 15.

Or we go deeper on the product. Map out exactly what the app looks like screen by screen — the full user flow from opening the app to a confirmed booking. This becomes your brief for a developer or for building it yourself with AI tools.

Or we work on the clinic pitch. You'll be walking into clinics soon. What do you actually say? We could build out a tight 2-minute pitch, a one-page leave-behind, and a simple demo script so you're ready.

Or we tackle the expat angle. This was your biggest competitive edge over OneDoc — we talked about it but never fully built it out. What does the expat onboarding flow actually look like screen by screen?

Q: Where do you want to start today? A: Map out the full app screen by screen

5:04 PM
Architected comprehensive app flowchart mapping user and clinic journeys

Good choice — this becomes the blueprint for everything. The developer brief, the pitch to clinics, the App Store screenshots, all of it flows from this.

Let me first map the full flow visually, then we'll go screen by screen in detail.

Let me build the full screen flow visually first, then we'll go deep on each one.

V

Connecting to visualize...

That's the full map. Every screen, every flow. Now let's go through each one in detail.

Onboarding

Splash screen — LOKKT logo on white, 1.5 seconds, then auto-advances. No skip button needed. First impression of the brand.

Sign up / Log in — phone number only. No email, no password. A verification SMS code gets sent. One screen, done in 20 seconds. Reduces drop-off massively compared to email signup flows.

Expat onboarding — only shown once, first time only. Three questions: what language do you prefer, are you new to Switzerland, what insurance do you have. Takes 15 seconds. Unlocks personalised results from the start.

Search & Discovery

Home screen — map fills 70% of the screen. Search bar pinned at the top. Three quick filter pills below it: "Available today", "Near me", "English-speaking". This is where most users spend their time.

Search results — toggle between map pins and a scrollable list. Each card shows clinic name, specialty, distance, next available slot, and star rating. Slots highlighted green if available today.

Filters — slide-up panel. Specialty type, language spoken, available date, insurance accepted, distance radius. Saves filter preferences for next time.

Clinic profile — photo at top, name, address, languages spoken, insurance types accepted. Below that — a grid of available time slots colour coded by how soon they are. Green = today, blue = this week, gray = later.

Booking Flow — 3 Taps

Pick a slot — tap any green or blue time block on the clinic profile. It highlights. One tap.

Confirm — shows a summary card: clinic name, date, time, service type. Face ID or passcode to confirm. One tap.

Booking confirmed — green checkmark, appointment details, add to calendar button. SMS and push notification sent automatically in the background. Done.

Post-Booking

My appointments — bottom nav tab. Upcoming at the top, past appointments below. Each card shows clinic, date, time, and a one-tap reschedule or cancel button.

Reschedule / cancel — tapping reschedule goes back to the clinic's availability grid with your original slot shown as taken. Pick a new one, confirm. Slot returns to pool automatically on cancel.

Reminders — automatic, not a screen. SMS at 24 hours, push notification at 2 hours. Non-negotiable — this is the core value for clinics.

Post-Visit

Rate the clinic — push notification 2 hours after appointment time: "How was your visit?" Five stars, optional one-line comment. Builds the trust layer that makes the platform self-governing over time.

Add to favourites — heart icon on any clinic profile. Favourited clinics appear at the top of search results next time. Makes rebooking a regular clinic instant.

Profile & Settings

Name, language preference, insurance type, notification settings. Simple and rarely visited — gets out of the way.

Clinic Dashboard — Web

Six screens, all functional and minimal. Availability manager, bookings list, analytics, profile editor, reminders log. We can go deep on any of these whenever you're ready.

Which section do you want to go deeper on — the patient booking flow, the expat onboarding, or the clinic dashboard?

Architected mobile-first app mockup showcasing modern healthcare interface design

Three full screens, all in your brand. Here's what each one shows:

Screen 1 — Home — the map takes up the top half with live clinic pins, your search bar and quick filter pills sit on top, and a bottom sheet slides up showing clinic cards with availability slots colour coded green for today, blue for this week.

Screen 2 — Clinic Profile — the clinic header in your electric blue, languages and insurance badges, then the slot grid where green means today and blue means this week. Tap any slot and you go to confirm.

Screen 3 — Confirm Booking — clean summary card showing date, time, location and type. The reminder note tells the patient they'll get notified at 24h and 2h. Face ID to confirm. One tap, done.

The user prompt is just "C" which appears to be incomplete or unclear. However, following the instructions, here is my summary: Orchestrated platform guidance and landing page design synthesis

Platform Advice First

For LOKKT's website, here's the honest recommendation:

Use Framer. Not Webflow, not Wix, not WordPress. Framer specifically because it produces the cleanest, most modern output, has built-in animations that match the kind of brand LOKKT is building, and you can launch a real domain in under a day. It also has a free tier to start. The learning curve is about 2-3 hours — it's drag and drop but with proper design control.

Your domain: Register lokkt.ch immediately on Infomaniak (Swiss registrar, CHF 15/year). Do this today before anyone else does.

The website has two jobs only:

  • Patients → download the app
  • Clinics → join the waitlist

One page. Two audiences. Everything else is noise at this stage.

Now let me build you the full landing page.

There's your full website. Here's what's inside:

5 sections, fully working:

Hero — bold headline, your tagline, stats bar showing 60s booking / 40% fewer no-shows / free for patients. Phone mockup on the right with live clinic cards.

How it works — three steps, Search → Pick a slot → Confirmed. Clean, minimal, scroll-animated.

Why LOKKT — five cards covering your biggest edges: expat onboarding, speed, mobile-first, Swiss data privacy, same-day booking.

For clinics — side-by-side layout with your three selling points and the pricing cards showing CHF 200 founding partner vs CHF 300 standard. Clinic form has a separate green CTA.

Waitlist — two-tab form, one for patients and one for clinics. The clinic tab collects name, role, email, phone, and specialty. Submit button confirms with a green tick.

Also includes:

  • Custom cursor that follows your mouse with a lag ring
  • Scroll-reveal animations on every card
  • Full mobile responsive layout

Restructured pricing and refined design to feel more human and authentic

Here's what changed from v1:

Clearer language throughout — no jargon, no startup buzzwords. "Three steps. You're booked." instead of "Three taps. You're booked." The copy reads like a real person wrote it, not a pitch deck.

Pricing fixed — CHF 199 and CHF 299. The standard plan doesn't look worse — it has the same core features, the founding partner just gets a cheaper rate locked in permanently plus the badge and priority placement. Both cards feel equally appealing, just different timing.

"You only pay when we launch" — this is now in three places: on the founding partner card, on the standard card, and in the clinic waitlist form note at the bottom. Very clear, no ambiguity.

Less AI-looking — removed the custom cursor, removed the noise overlay and heavy glow effects, switched to Lora for the italic headline which gives it a more editorial human feel. The layout is cleaner and uses more whitespace. Cards are less dramatic.

"Who it's for" section — replaced the abstract feature grid with two plain-English cards, one for patients and one for clinics. An older person reading this immediately knows which column is theirs.

6:05 PM
Orchestrated clinic setup guidance and redesigned dual-portal website architecture

Let me answer both questions first, then build the new site.

What The HTML File Is

It's a design template — not a live website. Think of it like a blueprint. You open it in your browser and it looks exactly like a real website, but it's just a file sitting on your computer. Nobody else can visit it.

Its purpose is to show you what LOKKT's website should look like so you can either hand it to a developer or recreate it in Framer. When you build the real site on Framer and publish it to lokkt.ch, that's when it becomes a real website the world can visit.

The Clinic Setup — How It Actually Works

This is the most important operational question you've asked. Here's exactly how it works end to end.

Your side — what you do:

You visit the clinic in person, sign them up on the waitlist, and get their letter of intent signed. Before launch, your developer builds a clinic dashboard — a simple web page at something like dashboard.lokkt.ch — where the clinic logs in and manages their availability.

When launch day comes, you or a team member physically sits with each clinic for 30 minutes and onboards them. You create their account, upload their profile photo, add their specialties, languages, and insurance types. Then you show them how to use the dashboard. That's the setup.

Their side — how availability actually works:

This is the key question. Clinics don't update their availability in real time manually — that would be impossible. Here's how it works in practice:

The clinic sets a recurring schedule template once. For example: "I'm available Monday to Friday, 9am to 12pm and 2pm to 6pm, appointments are 30 minutes each." LOKKT's system automatically generates all the available slots from that template — so Monday 9:00, 9:30, 10:00, 10:30 and so on appear automatically without anyone doing anything.

When a patient books a slot, it disappears from the available list instantly. When a patient cancels, it reappears automatically. The clinic never has to manually update anything for normal bookings.

The clinic only needs to touch the dashboard for exceptions — blocking a holiday, adding a half day, closing for a conference. That's maybe 5 minutes a week of their time.

The harder problem — existing bookings:

Here's the real complication. Most clinics already have patients booked through phone calls and their existing system. If Dr. Martin already has Tuesday 10am booked through his old system, LOKKT doesn't know that — and might show it as available.

There are two ways to handle this:

The simple way at launch: the clinic manually marks their already-booked slots as blocked in the LOKKT dashboard. This is a one-time 20-minute job when they onboard. Going forward, any booking that comes through phone or walk-in, they block that slot in LOKKT themselves. It's extra admin but manageable for small clinics.

The better way later: integrate with the clinic's existing software. Most clinics in Switzerland use one of a handful of systems — Medifile, Axonlab, or similar. If you build an API connection to those systems, LOKKT syncs automatically and the clinic never has to touch two systems. This is a later-stage feature, not something you need at launch.

The honest answer: At launch, availability is semi-manual. The clinic sets their template once, manually blocks slots that are already taken, and keeps LOKKT updated for walk-in bookings. It's a small amount of extra work but the value they get back — filled slots, fewer no-shows, no phone admin — is worth it. You make this very clear during onboarding so there are no surprises.

Now let me build the split website.

Created a file, read a file

Here's what's new in v3:

Entry page — when anyone visits lokkt.ch they see just the logo, tagline, and two cards. "I'm looking for a doctor" or "I run a clinic." One click and they go to the right place. Clean, instant, no confusion.

Two completely separate experiences — the patient side is blue-coded and focused on ease and speed. The clinic side is green-coded and focused on business value and no-shows. Different hero, different how-it-works, different why-us, different everything.

Why us block on both — five cards on the patient side covering language filtering, speed, same-day booking, reminders, and Swiss data privacy. Five cards on the clinic side covering no-shows, expat reach, less phone admin, analytics, and the pay-only-at-launch guarantee.

Pricing fixed — CHF 199 and CHF 299. Priority placement removed. Standard plan looks equally good — same core features, just without the founding partner badge, personal onboarding visit, and locked rate. The difference is clear without one feeling lesser.

60s with a bigger S — done.

Languages — French, German, Italian, English, and more throughout.

Nav switcher — at the top right, patients and clinics can always switch between the two sides without going back to the entry page.

Code · HTML 

i want the calander to be easier for the clinic, it cant be that they have to do all that, would there be a way to connect to their current one? think about it, there has to be a easier way, also how can i put this in framer

6:13 PM
Examined calendar integration options and Framer implementation strategy

The Calendar Problem — There's A Much Better Way

You're right to push back on this. Asking clinics to manually block slots is a deal-breaker — it's too much work and they'll churn fast.

Here's the insight: almost every clinic already uses a calendar. The solution isn't to replace it — it's to connect to it.

The Easiest Solution — iCal Sync

Every major calendar system — Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Outlook, and most medical practice software — can export something called an iCal feed. It's a standard link that any other system can read.

Here's how it works for LOKKT:

The clinic goes into their existing Google Calendar or Outlook, copies their iCal link — it takes 30 seconds — and pastes it into their LOKKT dashboard once. From that moment, LOKKT reads their calendar automatically. Every existing booking shows as blocked. Every new booking they add anywhere shows as blocked within minutes. They never touch LOKKT to manage existing bookings.

When a patient books through LOKKT, the slot gets added back into the clinic's Google Calendar automatically as a new appointment. So everything stays in one place — the calendar they already use.

The clinic's workflow changes by exactly zero. They keep using whatever they use today. LOKKT just sits on top and reads it.

What This Looks Like In Practice

Clinic's Google Calendar  ←→  LOKKT sync  ←→  Patient books on app
         ↓                                            ↓
   Already blocked                          New appointment appears
   slots hidden                             in Google Calendar
   from patients                            automatically

What Requires A Bit More Work — Practice Management Software

Some clinics use dedicated Swiss medical software like Medifile, Axonlab, or Winpraktika. These don't always have a simple iCal export. For these you'd need a proper API integration — more technical, more expensive, and something to build in v2 not at launch.

At launch, the pitch to clinics is: "Do you use Google Calendar, Outlook, or Apple Calendar? Then setup takes 2 minutes." Most small independent clinics in Geneva do. The ones on specialist practice software are a later problem.

Your Onboarding Script Becomes

  1. Clinic opens Google Calendar
  2. Settings → click their calendar → scroll to "Secret address in iCal format" → copy link
  3. Paste into LOKKT dashboard
  4. Done — their availability is live

That's the whole setup for availability. Everything else — profile, languages, insurance types — gets filled in together during the 30-minute onboarding call.

How To Put This In Framer

Here's exactly what to do, step by step:

Step 1 — Get Framer Go to framer.com, create a free account. The free plan lets you build and publish with a Framer subdomain. When you're ready to use lokkt.ch, that's the paid plan at around $20/month.

Step 2 — Don't copy the code directly Framer is a visual drag-and-drop tool. You can't paste raw HTML into it and get the same result. Instead, use the website file I gave you as your visual reference — open it in your browser, look at each section, and rebuild it in Framer using their components.

Step 3 — The fastest way to use what we built Framer has an AI feature — you describe what you want and it generates a starting point. Type something like:

"Dark blue healthcare booking app landing page, two audiences — patients and clinics, modern minimal design, electric blue #1A56DB accent colour, Syne font for headings"

It will generate something close. Then you adjust it section by section to match our design.

Step 4 — Build section by section Start with the entry page — it's the simplest. Two cards, a logo, a tagline. Get that right first. Then build the patient hero, then the clinic hero. Each section is its own Framer frame.

Step 5 — Connect your domain Buy lokkt.ch on Infomaniak today — seriously, do this today before anyone else does. Then in Framer, go to Publish → Custom Domain and follow their instructions to connect it. Takes about 10 minutes.

Step 6 — The waitlist forms Framer's built-in forms won't do much on their own. Connect them to Tally.so — it's a free form tool that plugs directly into Framer. Patient submissions and clinic submissions go to separate Tally forms, and you get an email every time someone signs up.

The honest order of operations: register lokkt.ch today, then spend a weekend building in Framer using our design as the reference. You don't need to code anything. Want me to write you the exact AI prompt to use in Framer to get the closest starting point?

is there any that lets me do it for free

6:26 PM

Yes — a few good options depending on what you want.

Free Options Ranked

1. Framer — best design quality, free tier exists The free plan lets you build and publish but your URL will be something like lokkt.framer.website instead of lokkt.ch. Everything works, it just has their branding in the URL. When you're ready to connect lokkt.ch you upgrade to paid. Good for building now and launching properly later.

2. Webflow — free tier, similar situation Free plan publishes to yoursite.webflow.io. More powerful than Framer for complex sites but steeper learning curve. Probably overkill for what you need right now.

3. Carrd — genuinely free for simple pages carrd.co is extremely simple — one page sites, very clean, actually free with a custom domain for $19 one time not per month. Not as beautiful as Framer but gets the job done for a waitlist page. Good if you just want something live fast.

4. GitHub Pages — completely free, custom domain included This is the best option if you want lokkt.ch for free. You upload the HTML file I built you directly to GitHub, connect your domain, and it's live. Completely free forever. The catch is it requires about 20 minutes of setup that feels slightly technical but I can walk you through every single step.

My Honest Recommendation

Short term: Use GitHub Pages with the HTML file I already built. It's free, you can connect lokkt.ch, and it's live in under an hour. No design work needed — the website is already done.

Medium term: Once you have clinic sign-ups coming in and some revenue, move to Framer for a more polished version.

Want me to walk you through putting the website live on GitHub Pages right now? It's genuinely simpler than it sounds.

that lets me do whatever i need to do

6:27 PM
Weighed platform options against user's website and form requirements

Honestly — none of them do everything completely free. But here's the closest you'll get:

The Free Stack That Actually Works

Website hosting — GitHub Pages (free) Hosts your website for free, forever. Custom domain like lokkt.ch works for free too. You just need to buy the domain itself — lokkt.ch on Infomaniak is about CHF 15/year. That's the only thing you pay for.

Waitlist forms — Tally.so (free) Tally is completely free for unlimited forms and unlimited responses. You build one form for patients, one for clinics, and every submission lands in your Tally dashboard and gets emailed to you. It connects to the website with a simple link or popup.

Design edits — directly in the HTML file (free) The website I built you is one HTML file. You can open it in any text editor — even Notepad — and change words, colours, and text without knowing how to code. Changing "Geneva" to "Lausanne" for example is just finding the word and replacing it.

Analytics — Google Analytics (free) Paste one line of code into the file and you can see exactly how many people visit, which page they land on, how long they stay, and where they come from.

The One Catch

Framer and Webflow let you drag and drop to redesign things visually. GitHub Pages doesn't — if you want to move a section or change the layout significantly you need to edit the code. For text changes, colour changes, and content updates it's fine. For major redesigns it gets harder.

What I'd Do Right Now — In Order

Today: Register lokkt.ch on Infomaniak — CHF 15, takes 10 minutes

This week: Set up GitHub Pages with the website I built — I walk you through every step, takes about 30 minutes

Same week: Create two Tally forms — one for patients, one for clinics — and link them into the website

That's it. You'd have a live, professional website at lokkt.ch with working waitlist forms, completely free except the domain.

Want to start right now? I'll walk you through GitHub Pages step by step — literally "click here, type this, paste that." No technical knowledge needed.

okay name the things that i can go to, dentists, physio, etc

6:33 PM

Here's every clinic type worth targeting in Geneva:

High Priority — Book most frequently

GP / General Practitioner — médecin généraliste People need these constantly, highest booking volume of anything

Dentist — dentiste Everyone needs a dentist, high repeat bookings, often hard to find one taking new patients

Physiotherapist — physiothérapeute Very high volume, people often need weekly appointments, great for repeat bookings

Medium Priority — Good volume, easy to pitch

Osteopath — ostéopathe Very popular in Geneva, lots of independent practitioners

Psychologist / Therapist — psychologue / thérapeute Huge demand especially post-Covid, booking friction is very high for this category

Dermatologist — dermatologue Long waiting lists, people desperate to find availability

Gynaecologist — gynécologue High demand, women often struggle to find English-speaking ones in Geneva

Ophthalmologist — ophtalmologue / eye doctor Regular checkups, prescription renewals, steady volume

Nutritionist — nutritionniste Growing category, lots of independent practitioners with no digital presence

Lower Priority — Smaller volume but easy wins

Optician — opticien Eye tests and fittings, steady but lower frequency

Podiatrist — podologue Niche but underserved online

Acupuncturist — acupuncteur Alternative medicine, practitioners rarely have good booking systems

Speech therapist — logopédiste Mostly for children, parents would love an easier booking system

Cardiologist — cardiologue Specialist, needs GP referral usually, lower direct booking volume

Endocrinologist — endocrinologue Specialist, lower volume

The Order I'd Approach Them In

Start with GPs, dentists and physios. These three alone cover probably 70% of all healthcare bookings in Geneva. Get 5 of each and you have a compelling platform before you've even touched any other category.

The pitch is slightly different for each:

GPs — lead with no-shows and expat patients who can't find an English-speaking doctor

Dentists — lead with filling last-minute cancellations and online visibility

Physios — lead with recurring bookings and reducing the phone calls for weekly appointment management

Want me to help you build the actual pitch script for each one?

do you think i should call the clinics or personally go to them?

2:21 PM

Personally go. Every time. Here's why.

Calling Doesn't Work For This

When you call a clinic cold, you get one of three things — the receptionist who has no authority to make decisions, voicemail, or a polite "send us an email" that goes nowhere. You're easy to ignore on the phone. You're much harder to ignore in person.

Also think about what you're asking them to do. You're asking a clinic to trust a brand new app with their patient bookings. That's not a decision anyone makes over a phone call from a stranger. It needs a face, a handshake, and a real conversation.

What In-Person Gets You That Calling Doesn't

You can read the room instantly. If the receptionist looks stressed and overwhelmed, your opening line is "how many booking calls do you get a day?" If the waiting room is half empty, your line is "are you finding it hard to fill slots at the moment?"

You can show the app on your phone in 60 seconds. Seeing it is worth more than any amount of explaining.

You leave something physical behind — a card, a one-pager — that sits on their desk for days after you've gone.

You find out immediately what their real objections are so you can handle them on the spot or come back better prepared next time.

The Practical Approach

Don't call ahead. Just walk in. Calling ahead gives them a chance to say no before they've even met you. Walk in during a quiet moment — Tuesday to Thursday mornings between 9 and 11am are usually best. Avoid Monday mornings and Friday afternoons.

Ask for the practice manager, not the doctor. The doctor is busy with patients and doesn't make admin decisions. The practice manager or head receptionist is your person.

Keep it under 5 minutes the first time. You're not closing the deal on visit one. You're planting the seed. Leave your card, ask if you can follow up with more information, and go. The second visit is where the real conversation happens.

Do 5 clinics a day minimum. Some days you'll get lucky and have a real conversation. Most days you'll leave a card and move on. That's normal — it's a numbers game at first.

One More Thing

The fact that you're young and doing this in person actually works in your favour in Geneva. Clinic owners respect hustle. Walking in with confidence and a clear product is memorable. Most software companies send cold emails — you're showing up. That alone sets you apart.

Want me to write you the exact script for what to say when you walk in?
